Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain.

In the context of neuropathic pain in the dorsal spinal cord (ipsilateral lumbar SDH after nerve injury/SNI, including rat models), Sox9 functions as a regulator of astrocytic metabolism and phenotype. Nerve injury triggers phosphorylation of Sox9 at site 181, enhancing its nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity, which upregulates Hk1 (hexokinase 1) in astrocytes. Elevated Hk1 increases glycolytic flux and lactate production, leading to increased histone lactylation (notably H3K9la) at promoters of neuroinflammatory genes (e.g., Gfap, C3, Cfb). This pSox9→Hk1→lactate→H3K9la cascade drives emergence of detrimental astrocyte subclusters (especially Astro1 and Astro2), increases neuronal excitability, and contributes to induction and maintenance of neuropathic pain.
References: Chen, Y., Liao, Y., Zhu, H., Wu, Z., Wan, Y., Feng, X., Fan, C., Zhan, D., Cai, H., Cheung, M., Cheung, C. W., & Liu, J. A. (2025). Sox9 regulation of hexokinase 1 controls neuroinflammatory astrocyte subtypes in a rat model of neuropathic pain. Nat Commun, 16(1), 10249. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-65092-5
